nimbledeps/pkgs2/nim-2.2.10-17ec440fdb89f8903db29a17898af590087d2b64/lib/pure/times.nim

1 #
2 #
3 # Nim's Runtime Library
4 # (c) Copyright 2018 Nim contributors
5 #
6 # See the file "copying.txt", included in this
7 # distribution, for details about the copyright.
8 #
9
10 ##[
11 The `times` module contains routines and types for dealing with time using
12 the `proleptic Gregorian calendar<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proleptic_Gregorian_calendar>`_.
13 It's also available for the
14 `JavaScript target <backends.html#backends-the-javascript-target>`_.
15
16 Although the `times` module supports nanosecond time resolution, the
17 resolution used by `getTime()` depends on the platform and backend
18 (JS is limited to millisecond precision).
19
20 Examples
21 ========
22
23 ```nim
24 import std/[times, os]
25 # Simple benchmarking
26 let time = cpuTime()
27 sleep(100) # Replace this with something to be timed
28 echo "Time taken: ", cpuTime() - time
29
30 # Current date & time
31 let now1 = now() # Current timestamp as a DateTime in local time
32 let now2 = now().utc # Current timestamp as a DateTime in UTC
33 let now3 = getTime() # Current timestamp as a Time
34
35 # Arithmetic using Duration
36 echo "One hour from now : ", now() + initDuration(hours = 1)
37 # Arithmetic using TimeInterval
38 echo "One year from now : ", now() + 1.years
39 echo "One month from now : ", now() + 1.months
40 ```
41
42 Parsing and Formatting Dates
43 ============================
44
45 The `DateTime` type can be parsed and formatted using the different
46 `parse` and `format` procedures.
47
48 ```nim
49 let dt = parse("2000-01-01", "yyyy-MM-dd")
50 echo dt.format("yyyy-MM-dd")
51 ```
52
53 The different format patterns that are supported are documented below.
54
55 =========== ================================================================================= ==============================================
56 Pattern Description Example
57 =========== ================================================================================= ==============================================
58 `d` Numeric value representing the day of the month, | `1/04/2012 -> 1`
59 it will be either one or two digits long. | `21/04/2012 -> 21`
60 `dd` Same as above, but is always two digits. | `1/04/2012 -> 01`
61 | `21/04/2012 -> 21`
62 `ddd` Three letter string which indicates the day of the week. | `Saturday -> Sat`
63 | `Monday -> Mon`
64 `dddd` Full string for the day of the week. | `Saturday -> Saturday`
65 | `Monday -> Monday`
66 `GG` The last two digits of the Iso Week-Year | `30/12/2012 -> 13`
67 `GGGG` The Iso week-calendar year padded to four digits | `30/12/2012 -> 2013`
68 `h` The hours in one digit if possible. Ranging from 1-12. | `5pm -> 5`
69 | `2am -> 2`
70 `hh` The hours in two digits always. If the hour is one digit, 0 is prepended. | `5pm -> 05`
71 | `11am -> 11`
72 `H` The hours in one digit if possible, ranging from 0-23. | `5pm -> 17`
73 | `2am -> 2`
74 `HH` The hours in two digits always. 0 is prepended if the hour is one digit. | `5pm -> 17`
75 | `2am -> 02`
76 `m` The minutes in one digit if possible. | `5:30 -> 30`
77 | `2:01 -> 1`
78 `mm` Same as above but always two digits, 0 is prepended if the minute is one digit. | `5:30 -> 30`
79 | `2:01 -> 01`
80 `M` The month in one digit if possible. | `September -> 9`
81 | `December -> 12`
82 `MM` The month in two digits always. 0 is prepended if the month value is one digit. | `September -> 09`
83 | `December -> 12`
84 `MMM` Abbreviated three-letter form of the month. | `September -> Sep`
85 | `December -> Dec`
86 `MMMM` Full month string, properly capitalized. | `September -> September`
87 `s` Seconds as one digit if possible. | `00:00:06 -> 6`
88 `ss` Same as above but always two digits. 0 is prepended if the second is one digit. | `00:00:06 -> 06`
89 `t` `A` when time is in the AM. `P` when time is in the PM. | `5pm -> P`
90 | `2am -> A`
91 `tt` Same as above, but `AM` and `PM` instead of `A` and `P` respectively. | `5pm -> PM`
92 | `2am -> AM`
93 `yy` The last two digits of the year. When parsing, the current century is assumed. | `2012 AD -> 12`
94 `yyyy` The year, padded to at least four digits. | `2012 AD -> 2012`
95 Is always positive, even when the year is BC. | `24 AD -> 0024`
96 When the year is more than four digits, '+' is prepended. | `24 BC -> 00024`
97 | `12345 AD -> +12345`
98 `YYYY` The year without any padding. | `2012 AD -> 2012`
99 Is always positive, even when the year is BC. | `24 AD -> 24`
100 | `24 BC -> 24`
101 | `12345 AD -> 12345`
102 `uuuu` The year, padded to at least four digits. Will be negative when the year is BC. | `2012 AD -> 2012`
103 When the year is more than four digits, '+' is prepended unless the year is BC. | `24 AD -> 0024`
104 | `24 BC -> -0023`
105 | `12345 AD -> +12345`
106 `UUUU` The year without any padding. Will be negative when the year is BC. | `2012 AD -> 2012`
107 | `24 AD -> 24`
108 | `24 BC -> -23`
109 | `12345 AD -> 12345`
110 `V` The Iso Week-Number as one or two digits | `3/2/2012 -> 5`
111 | `1/4/2012 -> 13`
112 `VV` The Iso Week-Number as two digits always. 0 is prepended if one digit. | `3/2/2012 -> 05`
113 | `1/4/2012 -> 13`
114 `z` Displays the timezone offset from UTC. | `UTC+7 -> +7`
115 | `UTC-5 -> -5`
116 `zz` Same as above but with leading 0. | `UTC+7 -> +07`
117 | `UTC-5 -> -05`
118 `zzz` Same as above but with `:mm` where *mm* represents minutes. | `UTC+7 -> +07:00`
119 | `UTC-5 -> -05:00`
120 `ZZZ` Same as above but with `mm` where *mm* represents minutes. | `UTC+7 -> +0700`
121 | `UTC-5 -> -0500`
122 `zzzz` Same as above but with `:ss` where *ss* represents seconds. | `UTC+7 -> +07:00:00`
123 | `UTC-5 -> -05:00:00`
124 `ZZZZ` Same as above but with `ss` where *ss* represents seconds. | `UTC+7 -> +070000`
125 | `UTC-5 -> -050000`
126 `g` Era: AD or BC | `300 AD -> AD`
127 | `300 BC -> BC`
128 `fff` Milliseconds display | `1000000 nanoseconds -> 1`
129 `ffffff` Microseconds display | `1000000 nanoseconds -> 1000`
130 `fffffffff` Nanoseconds display | `1000000 nanoseconds -> 1000000`
131 =========== ================================================================================= ==============================================
132
133 Other strings can be inserted by putting them in `''`. For example
134 `hh'->'mm` will give `01->56`. In addition to spaces,
135 the following characters can be inserted without quoting them:
136 `:` `-` `,` `.` `(` `)` `/` `[` `]`.
137 A literal `'` can be specified with `''`.
138
139 However you don't need to necessarily separate format patterns, as an
140 unambiguous format string like `yyyyMMddhhmmss` is also valid (although
141 only for years in the range 1..9999).
142
143 Duration vs TimeInterval
144 ============================
145 The `times` module exports two similar types that are both used to
146 represent some amount of time: `Duration <#Duration>`_ and
147 `TimeInterval <#TimeInterval>`_.
148 This section explains how they differ and when one should be preferred over the
149 other (short answer: use `Duration` unless support for months and years is
150 needed).
151
152 Duration
153 ----------------------------
154 A `Duration` represents a duration of time stored as seconds and
155 nanoseconds. A `Duration` is always fully normalized, so
156 `initDuration(hours = 1)` and `initDuration(minutes = 60)` are equivalent.
157
158 Arithmetic with a `Duration` is very fast, especially when used with the
159 `Time` type, since it only involves basic arithmetic. Because `Duration`
160 is more performant and easier to understand it should generally preferred.
161
162 TimeInterval
163 ----------------------------
164 A `TimeInterval` represents an amount of time expressed in calendar
165 units, for example "1 year and 2 days". Since some units cannot be
166 normalized (the length of a year is different for leap years for example),
167 the `TimeInterval` type uses separate fields for every unit. The
168 `TimeInterval`'s returned from this module generally don't normalize
169 **anything**, so even units that could be normalized (like seconds,
170 milliseconds and so on) are left untouched.
171
172 Arithmetic with a `TimeInterval` can be very slow, because it requires
173 timezone information.
174
175 Since it's slower and more complex, the `TimeInterval` type should be
176 avoided unless the program explicitly needs the features it offers that
177 `Duration` doesn't have.
178
179 How long is a day?
180 ----------------------------
181 It should be especially noted that the handling of days differs between
182 `TimeInterval` and `Duration`. The `Duration` type always treats a day
183 as exactly 86400 seconds. For `TimeInterval`, it's more complex.
184
185 As an example, consider the amount of time between these two timestamps, both
186 in the same timezone:
187
188 - 2018-03-25T12:00+02:00
189 - 2018-03-26T12:00+01:00
190
191 If only the date & time is considered, it appears that exactly one day has
192 passed. However, the UTC offsets are different, which means that the
193 UTC offset was changed somewhere in between. This happens twice each year for
194 timezones that use daylight savings time. Because of this change, the amount
195 of time that has passed is actually 25 hours.
196
197 The `TimeInterval` type uses calendar units, and will say that exactly one
198 day has passed. The `Duration` type on the other hand normalizes everything
199 to seconds, and will therefore say that 90000 seconds has passed, which is
200 the same as 25 hours.
201
202 See also
203 ========
204 * `monotimes module <monotimes.html>`_
205 ]##
206
207 import std/[strutils, math, options]
208
209 import std/private/since
210 include "system/inclrtl"
211
212 when defined(nimPreviewSlimSystem):
213 import std/assertions
214
215
216 when defined(js):
217 import std/jscore
218 import std/private/jsutils
219
220 when jsNoBigInt64:
221 # This is really bad, but overflow checks are broken badly for
222 # ints on the JS backend. See #6752.
223 {.push overflowChecks: off.}
224 proc `*`(a, b: int64): int64 =
225 system.`*`(a, b)
226 proc `*`(a, b: int): int =
227 system.`*`(a, b)
228 proc `+`(a, b: int64): int64 =
229 system.`+`(a, b)
230 proc `+`(a, b: int): int =
231 system.`+`(a, b)
232 proc `-`(a, b: int64): int64 =
233 system.`-`(a, b)
234 proc `-`(a, b: int): int =
235 system.`-`(a, b)
236 proc inc(a: var int, b: int) =
237 system.inc(a, b)
238 proc inc(a: var int64, b: int) =
239 system.inc(a, b)
240 {.pop.}
241
242 elif defined(posix):
243 import std/posix
244
245 type CTime = posix.Time
246
247 when defined(macosx):
248 proc gettimeofday(tp: var Timeval, unused: pointer = nil)
249 {.importc: "gettimeofday", header: "<sys/time.h>", sideEffect.}
250
251 elif defined(windows):
252 import std/winlean, std/time_t
253
254 type
255 CTime = time_t.Time
256 Tm {.importc: "struct tm", header: "<time.h>", final, pure.} = object
257 tm_sec*: cint ## Seconds [0,60].
258 tm_min*: cint ## Minutes [0,59].
259 tm_hour*: cint ## Hour [0,23].
260 tm_mday*: cint ## Day of month [1,31].
261 tm_mon*: cint ## Month of year [0,11].
262 tm_year*: cint ## Years since 1900.
263 tm_wday*: cint ## Day of week [0,6] (Sunday =0).
264 tm_yday*: cint ## Day of year [0,365].
265 tm_isdst*: cint ## Daylight Savings flag.
266
267 # Prefer 64-bit version always - time_t might be 32 or 64 bit depending on
268 # the setting of _USE_32BIT_TIME_T and we have no way of detecting which
269 # version is actually used by default:
270 # https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/reference/localtime-localtime32-localtime64
271 proc localtime(a1: var CTime): ptr Tm {.importc: "_localtime64", header: "<time.h>", sideEffect.}
272
273 type
274 Month* = enum ## Represents a month. Note that the enum starts at `1`,
275 ## so `ord(month)` will give the month number in the
276 ## range `1..12`.
277 mJan = (1, "January")
278 mFeb = "February"
279 mMar = "March"
280 mApr = "April"
281 mMay = "May"
282 mJun = "June"
283 mJul = "July"
284 mAug = "August"
285 mSep = "September"
286 mOct = "October"
287 mNov = "November"
288 mDec = "December"
289
290 WeekDay* = enum ## Represents a weekday.
291 dMon = "Monday"
292 dTue = "Tuesday"
293 dWed = "Wednesday"
294 dThu = "Thursday"
295 dFri = "Friday"
296 dSat = "Saturday"
297 dSun = "Sunday"
298
299 type
300 MonthdayRange* = range[1..31]
301 HourRange* = range[0..23]
302 MinuteRange* = range[0..59]
303 SecondRange* = range[0..60] ## \
304 ## Includes the value 60 to allow for a leap second. Note however
305 ## that the `second` of a `DateTime` will never be a leap second.
306 YeardayRange* = range[0..365]
307 NanosecondRange* = range[0..999_999_999]
308
309 IsoWeekRange* = range[1 .. 53]
310 ## An ISO 8601 calendar week number.
311 IsoYear* = distinct int
312 ## An ISO 8601 calendar year number.
313 ##
314 ## .. warning:: The ISO week-based year can correspond to the following or previous year from 29 December to January 3.
315
316 Time* = object ## Represents a point in time.
317 seconds: int64
318 nanosecond: NanosecondRange
319
320 DateTime* = object of RootObj ## \
321 ## Represents a time in different parts. Although this type can represent
322 ## leap seconds, they are generally not supported in this module. They are
323 ## not ignored, but the `DateTime`'s returned by procedures in this
324 ## module will never have a leap second.
325 nanosecond: NanosecondRange
326 second: SecondRange
327 minute: MinuteRange
328 hour: HourRange
329 monthdayZero: int
330 monthZero: int
331 year: int
332 weekday: WeekDay
333 yearday: YeardayRange
334 isDst: bool
335 timezone: Timezone
336 utcOffset: int
337
338 Duration* = object ## Represents a fixed duration of time, meaning a duration
339 ## that has constant length independent of the context.
340 ##
341 ## To create a new `Duration`, use `initDuration
342 ## <#initDuration,int64,int64,int64,int64,int64,int64,int64,int64>`_.
343 ## Instead of trying to access the private attributes, use
344 ## `inSeconds <#inSeconds,Duration>`_ for converting to seconds and
345 ## `inNanoseconds <#inNanoseconds,Duration>`_ for converting to nanoseconds.
346 seconds: int64
347 nanosecond: NanosecondRange
348
349 TimeUnit* = enum ## Different units of time.
350 Nanoseconds, Microseconds, Milliseconds, Seconds, Minutes, Hours, Days,
351 Weeks, Months, Years
352
353 FixedTimeUnit* = range[Nanoseconds..Weeks] ## \
354 ## Subrange of `TimeUnit` that only includes units of fixed duration.
355 ## These are the units that can be represented by a `Duration`.
356
357 TimeInterval* = object ## \
358 ## Represents a non-fixed duration of time. Can be used to add and
359 ## subtract non-fixed time units from a `DateTime <#DateTime>`_ or
360 ## `Time <#Time>`_.
361 ##
362 ## Create a new `TimeInterval` with `initTimeInterval proc
363 ## <#initTimeInterval,int,int,int,int,int,int,int,int,int,int>`_.
364 ##
365 ## Note that `TimeInterval` doesn't represent a fixed duration of time,
366 ## since the duration of some units depend on the context (e.g a year
367 ## can be either 365 or 366 days long). The non-fixed time units are
368 ## years, months, days and week.
369 ##
370 ## Note that `TimeInterval`'s returned from the `times` module are
371 ## never normalized. If you want to normalize a time unit,
372 ## `Duration <#Duration>`_ should be used instead.
373 nanoseconds*: int ## The number of nanoseconds
374 microseconds*: int ## The number of microseconds
375 milliseconds*: int ## The number of milliseconds
376 seconds*: int ## The number of seconds
377 minutes*: int ## The number of minutes
378 hours*: int ## The number of hours
379 days*: int ## The number of days
380 weeks*: int ## The number of weeks
381 months*: int ## The number of months
382 years*: int ## The number of years
383
384 Timezone* = ref object ## \
385 ## Timezone interface for supporting `DateTime <#DateTime>`_\s of arbitrary
386 ## timezones. The `times` module only supplies implementations for the
387 ## system's local time and UTC.
388 zonedTimeFromTimeImpl: proc (x: Time): ZonedTime
389 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.}
390 zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl: proc (x: Time): ZonedTime
391 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.}
392 name: string
393
394 ZonedTime* = object ## Represents a point in time with an associated
395 ## UTC offset and DST flag. This type is only used for
396 ## implementing timezones.
397 time*: Time ## The point in time being represented.
398 utcOffset*: int ## The offset in seconds west of UTC,
399 ## including any offset due to DST.
400 isDst*: bool ## Determines whether DST is in effect.
401
402 DurationParts* = array[FixedTimeUnit, int64] # Array of Duration parts starts
403 TimeIntervalParts* = array[TimeUnit, int] # Array of Duration parts starts
404
405 const
406 secondsInMin = 60
407 secondsInHour = 60*60
408 secondsInDay = 60*60*24
409 rateDiff = 10000000'i64 # 100 nsecs
410 # The number of hectonanoseconds between 1601/01/01 (windows epoch)
411 # and 1970/01/01 (unix epoch).
412 epochDiff = 116444736000000000'i64
413
414 const unitWeights: array[FixedTimeUnit, int64] = [
415 1'i64,
416 1000,
417 1_000_000,
418 1e9.int64,
419 secondsInMin * 1e9.int64,
420 secondsInHour * 1e9.int64,
421 secondsInDay * 1e9.int64,
422 7 * secondsInDay * 1e9.int64,
423 ]
424
425 when (NimMajor, NimMinor) >= (1, 4):
426 # Newer versions of Nim don't track defects
427 {.pragma: parseFormatRaises, raises: [TimeParseError, TimeFormatParseError].}
428 {.pragma: parseRaises, raises: [TimeParseError].}
429 else:
430 # Still track when using older versions
431 {.pragma: parseFormatRaises, raises: [TimeParseError, TimeFormatParseError, Defect].}
432 {.pragma: parseRaises, raises: [TimeParseError, Defect].}
433
434
435 #
436 # Helper procs
437 #
438
439 {.pragma: operator, rtl, noSideEffect, gcsafe.}
440
441 6712 proc convert*[T: SomeInteger](unitFrom, unitTo: FixedTimeUnit, quantity: T): T
442 {.inline.} =
443 ## Convert a quantity of some duration unit to another duration unit.
444 ## This proc only deals with integers, so the result might be truncated.
445 runnableExamples:
446 doAssert convert(Days, Hours, 2) == 48
447 doAssert convert(Days, Weeks, 13) == 1 # Truncated
448 doAssert convert(Seconds, Milliseconds, -1) == -1000

490 4 proc toEpochDay(monthday: MonthdayRange, month: Month, year: int): int64 =
491 ## Get the epoch day from a year/month/day date.
492 ## The epoch day is the number of days since 1970/01/01
493 ## (it might be negative).
494 # Based on https://howardhinnant.github.io/date_algorithms.html

544 2 proc getDayOfWeek*(monthday: MonthdayRange, month: Month, year: int): WeekDay
545 1 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.} =
546 ## Returns the day of the week enum from day, month and year.

548 runnableExamples:
549 doAssert getDayOfWeek(13, mJun, 1990) == dWed

565 1 doAssert getDaysInYear(2001) == 365
566 1 result = 365 + (if isLeapYear(year): 1 else: 0)
567
568 proc `==`*(a, b: IsoYear): bool {.borrow.}
569 proc `$`*(p: IsoYear): string {.borrow.}
570
571 ✗ proc getWeeksInIsoYear*(y: IsoYear): IsoWeekRange {.since: (1, 5).} =
572 ## Returns the number of weeks in the specified ISO 8601 week-based year, which can be
573 ## either 53 or 52.
574 runnableExamples:
575 assert getWeeksInIsoYear(IsoYear(2019)) == 52
576 assert getWeeksInIsoYear(IsoYear(2020)) == 53
577
578 var y = int(y)
579
580 # support negative years
581 ✗ y = if y < 0: 400 + y mod 400 else: y
582 ✗
583 ✗ # source: https://webspace.science.uu.nl/~gent0113/calendar/isocalendar.htm
584 let p = (y + (y div 4) - (y div 100) + (y div 400)) mod 7
585 ✗ let y1 = y - 1
586 ✗ let p1 = (y1 + (y1 div 4) - (y1 div 100) + (y1 div 400)) mod 7
587 ✗ if p == 4 or p1 == 3: 53 else: 52
588 ✗
589 ✗ proc getIsoWeekAndYear*(dt: DateTime):
590 ✗ tuple[isoweek: IsoWeekRange, isoyear: IsoYear] {.since: (1, 5).} =
591 ✗ ## Returns the ISO 8601 week and year.
592 ##
593 ✗ ## .. warning:: The ISO week-based year can correspond to the following or previous year from 29 December to January 3.
594 runnableExamples:
595 ✗ assert getIsoWeekAndYear(initDateTime(21, mApr, 2018, 00, 00, 00)) == (isoweek: 16.IsoWeekRange, isoyear: 2018.IsoYear)
596 ✗ block:
597 let (w, y) = getIsoWeekAndYear(initDateTime(30, mDec, 2019, 00, 00, 00))
598 assert w == 01.IsoWeekRange
599 assert y == 2020.IsoYear
600 assert getIsoWeekAndYear(initDateTime(13, mSep, 2020, 00, 00, 00)) == (isoweek: 37.IsoWeekRange, isoyear: 2020.IsoYear)
601 block:
602 let (w, y) = getIsoWeekAndYear(initDateTime(2, mJan, 2021, 00, 00, 00))
603 assert w.int > 52
604 assert w.int < 54
605 assert y.int mod 100 == 20
606
607 # source: https://webspace.science.uu.nl/~gent0113/calendar/isocalendar.htm
608 ✗ var w = (dt.yearday.int - dt.weekday.int + 10) div 7
609 ✗ if w < 1:
610 ✗ (isoweek: getWeeksInIsoYear(IsoYear(dt.year - 1)), isoyear: IsoYear(dt.year - 1))
611 ✗ elif (w > getWeeksInIsoYear(IsoYear(dt.year))):
612 ✗ (isoweek: IsoWeekRange(1), isoyear: IsoYear(dt.year + 1))
613 ✗ else:
614 ✗ (isoweek: IsoWeekRange(w), isoyear: IsoYear(dt.year))
615
616 ✗ proc stringifyUnit(value: int | int64, unit: TimeUnit): string =
617 ✗ ## Stringify time unit with it's name, lowercased
618 let strUnit = $unit
619 result = ""
620 result.addInt value
621 result.add ' '
622 if abs(value) != 1:
623 result.add(strUnit.toLowerAscii())
624 else:
625 result.add(strUnit[0..^2].toLowerAscii())
626
627 proc humanizeParts(parts: seq[string]): string =
628 ## Make date string parts human-readable
629 result = ""
630 if parts.len == 0:
631 result.add "0 nanoseconds"
632 elif parts.len == 1:
633 result = parts[0]
634 elif parts.len == 2:
635 result = parts[0] & " and " & parts[1]
636 else:
637 for i in 0..high(parts)-1:
638 result.add parts[i] & ", "
639 result.add "and " & parts[high(parts)]
640

651 29 template lqImpl(a: Duration|Time, b: Duration|Time): bool =
652 29 a.seconds < b.seconds or (
653 a.seconds == b.seconds and a.nanosecond <= b.nanosecond)
654
655 template eqImpl(a: Duration|Time, b: Duration|Time): bool =
656 a.seconds == b.seconds and a.nanosecond == b.nanosecond
657
658 #
659 # Duration
660 #
661
662 const DurationZero* = Duration() ## \
663 ## Zero value for durations. Useful for comparisons.
664 ## ```nim
665 ## doAssert initDuration(seconds = 1) > DurationZero
666 ## doAssert initDuration(seconds = 0) == DurationZero
667 ## ```
668
669 204 proc initDuration*(nanoseconds, microseconds, milliseconds,
670 seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks: int64 = 0): Duration =
671 ## Create a new `Duration <#Duration>`_.

673 let dur = initDuration(seconds = 1, milliseconds = 1)
674 doAssert dur.inMilliseconds == 1001
675 doAssert dur.inSeconds == 1

691 template convert(dur: Duration, unit: static[FixedTimeUnit]): int64 =
692 # The correction is required due to how durations are normalized.
693 # For example,` initDuration(nanoseconds = -1)` is stored as
694 # { seconds = -1, nanoseconds = 999999999 }.
695 when unit == Nanoseconds:
696 ✗ dur.seconds * 1_000_000_000 + dur.nanosecond
697 ✗ else:
698 47 let correction = dur.seconds < 0 and dur.nanosecond > 0
699 when unit >= Seconds:
700 convert(Seconds, unit, dur.seconds + ord(correction))
701 else:
702

✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 47 times.
 47 if correction:
703 ✗ convert(Seconds, unit, dur.seconds + 1) -
704 ✗ convert(Nanoseconds, unit,
705 ✗ convert(Seconds, Nanoseconds, 1) - dur.nanosecond)
706 else:
707

✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 47 times.
 47 convert(Seconds, unit, dur.seconds) +

✓ Branch 3 taken 47 times.
 47 convert(Nanoseconds, unit, dur.nanosecond)
709
710 47 proc inWeeks*(dur: Duration): int64 =
711 47 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole weeks.
712 runnableExamples:
713 let dur = initDuration(days = 8)
714 doAssert dur.inWeeks == 1
715 dur.convert(Weeks)
716
717 proc inDays*(dur: Duration): int64 =
718 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole days.
719 runnableExamples:
720 let dur = initDuration(hours = -50)
721 doAssert dur.inDays == -2
722 dur.convert(Days)
723
724 proc inHours*(dur: Duration): int64 =
725 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole hours.
726 runnableExamples:
727 let dur = initDuration(minutes = 60, days = 2)
728 doAssert dur.inHours == 49
729 dur.convert(Hours)
730
731 proc inMinutes*(dur: Duration): int64 =
732 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole minutes.
733 runnableExamples:
734 let dur = initDuration(hours = 2, seconds = 10)
735 doAssert dur.inMinutes == 120
736 dur.convert(Minutes)
737
738 proc inSeconds*(dur: Duration): int64 =
739 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole seconds.
740 runnableExamples:
741 let dur = initDuration(hours = 2, milliseconds = 10)
742 doAssert dur.inSeconds == 2 * 60 * 60
743 dur.convert(Seconds)
744
745 94 proc inMilliseconds*(dur: Duration): int64 =
746 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole milliseconds.
747 runnableExamples:
748 let dur = initDuration(seconds = -2)
749 doAssert dur.inMilliseconds == -2000
750
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 47 times.
 47 dur.convert(Milliseconds)
751 ✗
752 47 proc inMicroseconds*(dur: Duration): int64 =
753 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole microseconds.
754 runnableExamples:
755 let dur = initDuration(seconds = -2)
756 doAssert dur.inMicroseconds == -2000000
757 dur.convert(Microseconds)
758
759 ✗ proc inNanoseconds*(dur: Duration): int64 =
760 ## Converts the duration to the number of whole nanoseconds.
761 runnableExamples:
762 let dur = initDuration(seconds = -2)
763 doAssert dur.inNanoseconds == -2000000000
764 ✗ dur.convert(Nanoseconds)
765
766 proc toParts*(dur: Duration): DurationParts =
767 ## Converts a duration into an array consisting of fixed time units.
768 ##
769 ## Each value in the array gives information about a specific unit of
770 ## time, for example `result[Days]` gives a count of days.
771 ##
772 ## This procedure is useful for converting `Duration` values to strings.
773 runnableExamples:
774 var dp = toParts(initDuration(weeks = 2, days = 1))
775 doAssert dp[Days] == 1
776 doAssert dp[Weeks] == 2
777 doAssert dp[Minutes] == 0
778 dp = toParts(initDuration(days = -1))
779 doAssert dp[Days] == -1
780
781 var remS = dur.seconds
782 var remNs = dur.nanosecond.int
783
784 # Ensure the same sign for seconds and nanoseconds
785 if remS < 0 and remNs != 0:
786 remNs -= convert(Seconds, Nanoseconds, 1)
787 remS.inc 1
788
789 for unit in countdown(Weeks, Seconds):
790 let quantity = convert(Seconds, unit, remS)
791 remS = remS mod convert(unit, Seconds, 1)
792
793 result[unit] = quantity
794
795 for unit in countdown(Milliseconds, Nanoseconds):
796 let quantity = convert(Nanoseconds, unit, remNs)
797 remNs = remNs mod convert(unit, Nanoseconds, 1)
798
799 result[unit] = quantity
800
801 proc `$`*(dur: Duration): string =
802 ## Human friendly string representation of a `Duration`.
803 runnableExamples:
804 doAssert $initDuration(seconds = 2) == "2 seconds"
805 doAssert $initDuration(weeks = 1, days = 2) == "1 week and 2 days"
806 doAssert $initDuration(hours = 1, minutes = 2, seconds = 3) ==
807 "1 hour, 2 minutes, and 3 seconds"
808 doAssert $initDuration(milliseconds = -1500) ==
809 "-1 second and -500 milliseconds"
810 var parts = newSeq[string]()
811 var numParts = toParts(dur)
812
813 for unit in countdown(Weeks, Nanoseconds):
814 let quantity = numParts[unit]
815 if quantity != 0.int64:
816 parts.add(stringifyUnit(quantity, unit))
817
818 result = humanizeParts(parts)
819
820 406 proc `+`*(a, b: Duration): Duration {.operator, extern: "ntAddDuration".} =
821 ## Add two durations together.
822 runnableExamples:
823 doAssert initDuration(seconds = 1) + initDuration(days = 1) ==
824 initDuration(seconds = 1, days = 1)
825
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 406 times.
 812 addImpl[Duration](a, b)
826 406
827 proc `-`*(a, b: Duration): Duration {.operator, extern: "ntSubDuration".} =
828 ## Subtract a duration from another.
829 runnableExamples:
830 doAssert initDuration(seconds = 1, days = 1) - initDuration(seconds = 1) ==
831 initDuration(days = 1)
832 subImpl[Duration](a, b)
833
834 proc `-`*(a: Duration): Duration {.operator, extern: "ntReverseDuration".} =
835 ## Reverse a duration.
836 runnableExamples:
837 doAssert -initDuration(seconds = 1) == initDuration(seconds = -1)
838 normalize[Duration](-a.seconds, -a.nanosecond)
839
840 29 proc `<`*(a, b: Duration): bool {.operator, extern: "ntLtDuration".} =
841 ## Note that a duration can be negative,
842 ## so even if `a < b` is true `a` might
843 ## represent a larger absolute duration.
844 ## Use `abs(a) < abs(b)` to compare the absolute
845 ## duration.
846 runnableExamples:
847 doAssert initDuration(seconds = 1) < initDuration(seconds = 2)
848 doAssert initDuration(seconds = -2) < initDuration(seconds = 1)
849 doAssert initDuration(seconds = -2).abs < initDuration(seconds = 1).abs == false
850 29 ltImpl(a, b)
851
852 proc `<=`*(a, b: Duration): bool {.operator, extern: "ntLeDuration".} =
853 lqImpl(a, b)
854
855 proc `==`*(a, b: Duration): bool {.operator, extern: "ntEqDuration".} =
856 runnableExamples:
857 let
858 d1 = initDuration(weeks = 1)
859 d2 = initDuration(days = 7)
860 doAssert d1 == d2
861 eqImpl(a, b)
862
863 proc `*`*(a: int64, b: Duration): Duration {.operator,
864 extern: "ntMulInt64Duration".} =
865 ## Multiply a duration by some scalar.
866 runnableExamples:
867 doAssert 5 * initDuration(seconds = 1) == initDuration(seconds = 5)
868 doAssert 3 * initDuration(minutes = 45) == initDuration(hours = 2, minutes = 15)
869 normalize[Duration](a * b.seconds, a * b.nanosecond)
870
871 proc `*`*(a: Duration, b: int64): Duration {.operator,
872 extern: "ntMulDuration".} =
873 ## Multiply a duration by some scalar.
874 runnableExamples:
875 doAssert initDuration(seconds = 1) * 5 == initDuration(seconds = 5)
876 doAssert initDuration(minutes = 45) * 3 == initDuration(hours = 2, minutes = 15)
877 b * a
878
879 203 proc `+=`*(d1: var Duration, d2: Duration) =
880
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 203 times.
 203 d1 = d1 + d2
881 203
882 proc `-=`*(dt: var Duration, ti: Duration) =
883 dt = dt - ti
884
885 proc `*=`*(a: var Duration, b: int) =
886 a = a * b
887
888 proc `div`*(a: Duration, b: int64): Duration {.operator,
889 extern: "ntDivDuration".} =
890 ## Integer division for durations.
891 runnableExamples:
892 doAssert initDuration(seconds = 3) div 2 ==
893 initDuration(milliseconds = 1500)
894 doAssert initDuration(minutes = 45) div 30 ==
895 initDuration(minutes = 1, seconds = 30)
896 doAssert initDuration(nanoseconds = 3) div 2 ==
897 initDuration(nanoseconds = 1)
898 let carryOver = convert(Seconds, Nanoseconds, a.seconds mod b)
899 normalize[Duration](a.seconds div b, (a.nanosecond + carryOver) div b)
900
901 proc high*(typ: typedesc[Duration]): Duration =
902 ## Get the longest representable duration.
903 initDuration(seconds = high(int64), nanoseconds = high(NanosecondRange))
904
905 proc low*(typ: typedesc[Duration]): Duration =
906 ## Get the longest representable duration of negative direction.
907 initDuration(seconds = low(int64))
908
909 proc abs*(a: Duration): Duration =
910 runnableExamples:
911 doAssert initDuration(milliseconds = -1500).abs ==
912 initDuration(milliseconds = 1500)
913 initDuration(seconds = abs(a.seconds), nanoseconds = -a.nanosecond)
914
915 #
916 # Time
917 #
918
919 1 proc initTime*(unix: int64, nanosecond: NanosecondRange): Time =
920 ## Create a `Time <#Time>`_ from a unix timestamp and a nanosecond part.
921 3 result = Time(seconds: unix, nanosecond: nanosecond)
922 1
923 1 proc nanosecond*(time: Time): NanosecondRange =
924 ## Get the fractional part of a `Time` as the number
925 ## of nanoseconds of the second.
926 1 time.nanosecond
927
928 proc fromUnix*(unix: int64): Time
929 {.gcsafe, tags: [], raises: [], noSideEffect.} =
930 ## Convert a unix timestamp (seconds since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`)
931 ## to a `Time`.
932 runnableExamples:
933 doAssert $fromUnix(0).utc == "1970-01-01T00:00:00Z"
934 initTime(unix, 0)
935
936 2 proc toUnix*(t: Time): int64 {.gcsafe, tags: [], raises: [], noSideEffect.} =
937 ## Convert `t` to a unix timestamp (seconds since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`).
938 ## See also `toUnixFloat` for subsecond resolution.
939 runnableExamples:
940 doAssert fromUnix(0).toUnix() == 0
941 2 t.seconds
942
943 proc fromUnixFloat(seconds: float): Time {.gcsafe, tags: [], raises: [], noSideEffect.} =
944 ## Convert a unix timestamp in seconds to a `Time`; same as `fromUnix`
945 ## but with subsecond resolution.
946 runnableExamples:
947 doAssert fromUnixFloat(123456.0) == fromUnixFloat(123456)
948 doAssert fromUnixFloat(-123456.0) == fromUnixFloat(-123456)
949 let secs = seconds.floor
950 let nsecs = (seconds - secs) * 1e9
951 initTime(secs.int64, nsecs.NanosecondRange)
952
953 proc toUnixFloat(t: Time): float {.gcsafe, tags: [], raises: [].} =
954 ## Same as `toUnix` but using subsecond resolution.
955 runnableExamples:
956 let t = getTime()
957 # `<` because of rounding errors
958 doAssert abs(t.toUnixFloat().fromUnixFloat - t) < initDuration(nanoseconds = 1000)
959 t.seconds.float + t.nanosecond / convert(Seconds, Nanoseconds, 1)
960
961 since((1, 1)):
962 export fromUnixFloat
963 export toUnixFloat
964
965
966 proc fromWinTime*(win: int64): Time =
967 ## Convert a Windows file time (100-nanosecond intervals since
968 ## `1601-01-01T00:00:00Z`) to a `Time`.
969 const hnsecsPerSec = convert(Seconds, Nanoseconds, 1) div 100
970 let nanos = floorMod(win, hnsecsPerSec) * 100
971 let seconds = floorDiv(win - epochDiff, hnsecsPerSec)
972 result = initTime(seconds, nanos)
973
974 proc toWinTime*(t: Time): int64 =
975 ## Convert `t` to a Windows file time (100-nanosecond intervals
976 ## since `1601-01-01T00:00:00Z`).
977 result = t.seconds * rateDiff + epochDiff + t.nanosecond div 100
978
979 proc getTimeImpl(typ: typedesc[Time]): Time =
980 raiseAssert "implemented in the vm"
981
982 proc getTime*(): Time {.tags: [TimeEffect], gcsafe.} =
983 ## Gets the current time as a `Time` with up to nanosecond resolution.
984 when nimvm:
985 result = getTimeImpl(Time)
986 else:
987 when defined(js):
988 let millis = newDate().getTime()
989 let seconds = convert(Milliseconds, Seconds, millis)
990 let nanos = convert(Milliseconds, Nanoseconds,
991 millis mod convert(Seconds, Milliseconds, 1).int)
992 result = initTime(seconds, nanos)
993 elif defined(macosx):
994 var a {.noinit.}: Timeval
995 gettimeofday(a)
996 result = initTime(a.tv_sec.int64,
997 convert(Microseconds, Nanoseconds, a.tv_usec.int))
998 elif defined(posix):
999 var ts {.noinit.}: Timespec
1000 discard clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME, ts)
1001 result = initTime(ts.tv_sec.int64, ts.tv_nsec.int)
1002 elif defined(windows):
1003 var f {.noinit.}: FILETIME
1004 getSystemTimeAsFileTime(f)
1005 result = fromWinTime(rdFileTime(f))
1006
1007 proc `-`*(a, b: Time): Duration {.operator, extern: "ntDiffTime".} =
1008 ## Computes the duration between two points in time.
1009 runnableExamples:
1010 doAssert initTime(1000, 100) - initTime(500, 20) ==
1011 initDuration(minutes = 8, seconds = 20, nanoseconds = 80)
1012 subImpl[Duration](a, b)
1013
1014 proc `+`*(a: Time, b: Duration): Time {.operator, extern: "ntAddTime".} =
1015 ## Add a duration of time to a `Time`.
1016 runnableExamples:
1017 doAssert (fromUnix(0) + initDuration(seconds = 1)) == fromUnix(1)
1018 addImpl[Time](a, b)
1019
1020 1 proc `-`*(a: Time, b: Duration): Time {.operator, extern: "ntSubTime".} =
1021 ## Subtracts a duration of time from a `Time`.
1022 runnableExamples:
1023 doAssert (fromUnix(0) - initDuration(seconds = 1)) == fromUnix(-1)
1024
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 2 subImpl[Time](a, b)
1025 1
1026 proc `<`*(a, b: Time): bool {.operator, extern: "ntLtTime".} =
1027 ## Returns true if `a < b`, that is if `a` happened before `b`.
1028 runnableExamples:
1029 doAssert initTime(50, 0) < initTime(99, 0)
1030 ltImpl(a, b)
1031
1032 proc `<=`*(a, b: Time): bool {.operator, extern: "ntLeTime".} =
1033 ## Returns true if `a <= b`.
1034 lqImpl(a, b)
1035
1036 proc `==`*(a, b: Time): bool {.operator, extern: "ntEqTime".} =
1037 ## Returns true if `a == b`, that is if both times represent the same point in time.
1038 eqImpl(a, b)
1039
1040 proc `+=`*(t: var Time, b: Duration) =
1041 t = t + b
1042
1043 proc `-=`*(t: var Time, b: Duration) =
1044 t = t - b
1045
1046 proc high*(typ: typedesc[Time]): Time =
1047 initTime(high(int64), high(NanosecondRange))
1048
1049 proc low*(typ: typedesc[Time]): Time =
1050 initTime(0, 0)
1051
1052 #
1053 # DateTime & Timezone
1054 #
1055
1056 template assertDateTimeInitialized(dt: DateTime) =
1057 assert dt.monthdayZero != 0, "Uninitialized datetime"
1058
1059 proc nanosecond*(dt: DateTime): NanosecondRange {.inline.} =
1060 ## The number of nanoseconds after the second,
1061 ## in the range 0 to 999_999_999.
1062 assertDateTimeInitialized(dt)
1063 dt.nanosecond
1064
1065 proc second*(dt: DateTime): SecondRange {.inline.} =
1066 ## The number of seconds after the minute,
1067 ## in the range 0 to 59.
1068 assertDateTimeInitialized(dt)
1069 dt.second
1070
1071 proc minute*(dt: DateTime): MinuteRange {.inline.} =
1072 ## The number of minutes after the hour,
1073 ## in the range 0 to 59.
1074 assertDateTimeInitialized(dt)
1075 dt.minute
1076
1077 proc hour*(dt: DateTime): HourRange {.inline.} =
1078 ## The number of hours past midnight,
1079 ## in the range 0 to 23.

1115 proc isDst*(dt: DateTime): bool {.inline.} =
1116 ## Determines whether DST is in effect.
1117 ## Always false for the JavaScript backend.
1118 assertDateTimeInitialized(dt)
1119 dt.isDst

1127 3 proc utcOffset*(dt: DateTime): int {.inline.} =
1128 ✗ ## The offset in seconds west of UTC, including
1129 ✗ ## any offset due to DST. Note that the sign of
1130 ✗ ## this number is the opposite of the one in a
1131 1 ## formatted offset string like `+01:00` (which
1132 ## would be equivalent to the UTC offset
1133 ## `-3600`).
1134 assertDateTimeInitialized(dt)
1135 dt.utcOffset
1136
1137 proc isInitialized(dt: DateTime): bool =
1138 # Returns true if `dt` is not the (invalid) default value for `DateTime`.
1139 runnableExamples:
1140 doAssert now().isInitialized
1141 doAssert not default(DateTime).isInitialized
1142 dt.monthZero != 0
1143
1144 since((1, 3)):
1145 export isInitialized
1146
1147 proc isLeapDay*(dt: DateTime): bool {.since: (1, 1).} =
1148 ## Returns whether `t` is a leap day, i.e. Feb 29 in a leap year. This matters
1149 ## as it affects time offset calculations.
1150 runnableExamples:
1151 let dt = dateTime(2020, mFeb, 29, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1152 doAssert dt.isLeapDay
1153 doAssert dt+1.years-1.years != dt
1154 let dt2 = dateTime(2020, mFeb, 28, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1155 doAssert not dt2.isLeapDay
1156 doAssert dt2+1.years-1.years == dt2
1157 doAssertRaises(Exception): discard dateTime(2021, mFeb, 29, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1158 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
1159 dt.year.isLeapYear and dt.month == mFeb and dt.monthday == 29
1160
1161 proc toTime*(dt: DateTime): Time {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.} =
1162 ## Converts a `DateTime` to a `Time` representing the same point in time.
1163 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
1164 let epochDay = toEpochDay(dt.monthday, dt.month, dt.year)
1165 var seconds = epochDay * secondsInDay
1166 seconds.inc dt.hour * secondsInHour
1167 seconds.inc dt.minute * 60
1168 seconds.inc dt.second
1169 seconds.inc dt.utcOffset
1170 result = initTime(seconds, dt.nanosecond)
1171
1172 2 proc initDateTime(zt: ZonedTime, zone: Timezone): DateTime =
1173 ## Create a new `DateTime` using `ZonedTime` in the specified timezone.
1174 let adjTime = zt.time - initDuration(seconds = zt.utcOffset)
1175 let s = adjTime.seconds
1176 let epochday = floorDiv(s, secondsInDay)
1177 var rem = s - epochday * secondsInDay
1178 let hour = rem div secondsInHour
1179

1201 2 proc newTimezone*(
1202 name: string,
1203 zonedTimeFromTimeImpl: proc (time: Time): ZonedTime
1204 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.},
1205 zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl: proc (adjTime: Time): ZonedTime
1206 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.}
1207 ): owned Timezone =
1208 ## Create a new `Timezone`.
1209 ##
1210 ## `zonedTimeFromTimeImpl` and `zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl` is used
1211 ## as the underlying implementations for `zonedTimeFromTime` and
1212 ## `zonedTimeFromAdjTime`.
1213 ##
1214 ## If possible, the name parameter should match the name used in the
1215 ## tz database. If the timezone doesn't exist in the tz database, or if the
1216 ## timezone name is unknown, then any string that describes the timezone
1217 ## unambiguously can be used. Note that the timezones name is used for
1218 ## checking equality!
1219 runnableExamples:
1220 proc utcTzInfo(time: Time): ZonedTime =
1221 ZonedTime(utcOffset: 0, isDst: false, time: time)
1222 let utc = newTimezone("Etc/UTC", utcTzInfo, utcTzInfo)
1223
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 2 times.
 5 Timezone(
1224 3 name: name,
1225 1 zonedTimeFromTimeImpl: zonedTimeFromTimeImpl,
1226 2 zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl: zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl
1227 2 )
1228
1229 proc name*(zone: Timezone): string =
1230 ## The name of the timezone.
1231 ##
1232 ## If possible, the name will be the name used in the tz database.
1233 ## If the timezone doesn't exist in the tz database, or if the timezone
1234 ## name is unknown, then any string that describes the timezone
1235 ## unambiguously might be used. For example, the string "LOCAL" is used
1236 ## for the system's local timezone.
1237 ##
1238 ## See also: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database
1239 zone.name
1240
1241 1 proc zonedTimeFromTime*(zone: Timezone, time: Time): ZonedTime =
1242 ## Returns the `ZonedTime` for some point in time.
1243
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1 zone.zonedTimeFromTimeImpl(time)
1244
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1
1245 1 proc zonedTimeFromAdjTime*(zone: Timezone, adjTime: Time): ZonedTime =
1246 ## Returns the `ZonedTime` for some local time.
1247 ##
1248 ## Note that the `Time` argument does not represent a point in time, it
1249 ## represent a local time! E.g if `adjTime` is `fromUnix(0)`, it should be
1250 ## interpreted as 1970-01-01T00:00:00 in the `zone` timezone, not in UTC.
1251 zone.zonedTimeFromAdjTimeImpl(adjTime)
1252
1253 proc `$`*(zone: Timezone): string =
1254 ## Returns the name of the timezone.
1255 if zone != nil: result = zone.name
1256 else: result = ""
1257
1258 2 proc `==`*(zone1, zone2: Timezone): bool =
1259 ## Two `Timezone`'s are considered equal if their name is equal.
1260 runnableExamples:
1261 doAssert local() == local()
1262 doAssert local() != utc()

1269 1 proc inZone*(time: Time, zone: Timezone): DateTime
1270 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.} =
1271 ## Convert `time` into a `DateTime` using `zone` as the timezone.
1272 1 result = initDateTime(zone.zonedTimeFromTime(time), zone)
1273

✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1

✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1 proc inZone*(dt: DateTime, zone: Timezone): DateTime
1275 1 {.tags: [], raises: [], gcsafe.} =
1276 ## Returns a `DateTime` representing the same point in time as `dt` but
1277 ## using `zone` as the timezone.
1278 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
1279 dt.toTime.inZone(zone)
1280
1281 proc toAdjTime(dt: DateTime): Time =
1282 let epochDay = toEpochDay(dt.monthday, dt.month, dt.year)
1283 var seconds = epochDay * secondsInDay
1284 seconds.inc dt.hour * secondsInHour
1285 seconds.inc dt.minute * secondsInMin
1286 seconds.inc dt.second
1287 result = initTime(seconds, dt.nanosecond)
1288
1289 when defined(js):
1290 proc localZonedTimeFromTime(time: Time): ZonedTime {.gcsafe.} =
1291 let jsDate = newDate(time.seconds * 1000)
1292 let offset = jsDate.getTimezoneOffset() * secondsInMin
1293 result.time = time
1294 result.utcOffset = offset
1295 result.isDst = false
1296
1297 proc localZonedTimeFromAdjTime(adjTime: Time): ZonedTime {.gcsafe.} =
1298 let utcDate = newDate(adjTime.seconds * 1000)
1299 let localDate = newDate(utcDate.getUTCFullYear(), utcDate.getUTCMonth(),
1300 utcDate.getUTCDate(), utcDate.getUTCHours(), utcDate.getUTCMinutes(),
1301 utcDate.getUTCSeconds(), 0)
1302
1303 # This is as dumb as it looks - JS doesn't support years in the range
1304 # 0-99 in the constructor because they are assumed to be 19xx...
1305 # Because JS doesn't support timezone history,
1306 # it doesn't really matter in practice.
1307 if utcDate.getUTCFullYear() in 0 .. 99:
1308 localDate.setFullYear(utcDate.getUTCFullYear())
1309
1310 result.utcOffset = localDate.getTimezoneOffset() * secondsInMin
1311 result.time = adjTime + initDuration(seconds = result.utcOffset)
1312 result.isDst = false
1313
1314 else:

1323 2 proc getLocalOffsetAndDst(unix: int64): tuple[offset: int, dst: bool] =
1324 # Windows can't handle unix < 0, so we fall back to unix = 0.
1325 # FIXME: This should be improved by falling back to the WinAPI instead.
1326 when defined(windows):
1327 if unix < 0:
1328 var a = 0.CTime
1329 let tmPtr = localtime(a)
1330 if not tmPtr.isNil:
1331 let tm = tmPtr[]
1332 return ((0 - tm.toAdjUnix).int, false)
1333 return (0, false)
1334
1335 # In case of a 32-bit time_t, we fallback to the closest available
1336 # timezone information.
1337 var a = clamp(unix, low(CTime).int64, high(CTime).int64).CTime
1338 1 let tmPtr = localtime(a)
1339 1 if not tmPtr.isNil:
1340
1/2
✓ Branch 0 taken 1 time.
✗ Branch 1 not taken.
 1 let tm = tmPtr[]

✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
✗ Branch 2 not taken.
✓ Branch 3 taken 1 time.
 1 return ((a.int64 - tm.toAdjUnix).int, tm.tm_isdst > 0)

1344 2 proc localZonedTimeFromTime(time: Time): ZonedTime {.gcsafe.} =
1345
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1 let (offset, dst) = getLocalOffsetAndDst(time.seconds)
1346 1 result = ZonedTime(time: time, utcOffset: offset, isDst: dst)
1347
1348 ✗ proc localZonedTimeFromAdjTime(adjTime: Time): ZonedTime {.gcsafe.} =
1349 var adjUnix = adjTime.seconds
1350 let past = adjUnix - secondsInDay
1351 let (pastOffset, _) = getLocalOffsetAndDst(past)
1352
1353 let future = adjUnix + secondsInDay
1354 let (futureOffset, _) = getLocalOffsetAndDst(future)
1355
1356 var utcOffset: int
1357 ✗ if pastOffset == futureOffset:
1358 ✗ utcOffset = pastOffset.int
1359 ✗ else:
1360 ✗ if pastOffset > futureOffset:
1361 ✗ adjUnix -= secondsInHour
1362
1363 ✗ adjUnix += pastOffset
1364 ✗ utcOffset = getLocalOffsetAndDst(adjUnix).offset
1365
1366 ✗ # This extra roundtrip is needed to normalize any impossible datetimes
1367 # as a result of offset changes (normally due to dst)
1368 let utcUnix = adjTime.seconds + utcOffset
1369 ✗ let (finalOffset, dst) = getLocalOffsetAndDst(utcUnix)
1370 ✗ result.time = initTime(utcUnix, adjTime.nanosecond)
1371 ✗ result.utcOffset = finalOffset
1372 ✗ result.isDst = dst
1373 ✗
1374 proc utcTzInfo(time: Time): ZonedTime =
1375 ZonedTime(utcOffset: 0, isDst: false, time: time)
1376
1377 var utcInstance {.threadvar.}: Timezone
1378 var localInstance {.threadvar.}: Timezone
1379
1380 proc utc*(): Timezone =
1381 ## Get the `Timezone` implementation for the UTC timezone.
1382 runnableExamples:
1383 doAssert now().utc.timezone == utc()
1384 doAssert utc().name == "Etc/UTC"
1385 if utcInstance.isNil:
1386 utcInstance = newTimezone("Etc/UTC", utcTzInfo, utcTzInfo)
1387 result = utcInstance
1388
1389 1 proc local*(): Timezone =
1390 ## Get the `Timezone` implementation for the local timezone.
1391 runnableExamples:
1392 doAssert now().timezone == local()
1393 doAssert local().name == "LOCAL"
1394
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1 if localInstance.isNil:
1395
1/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
✓ Branch 1 taken 1 time.
 1 localInstance = newTimezone("LOCAL", localZonedTimeFromTime,
1396 localZonedTimeFromAdjTime)
1397 result = localInstance
1398
1399 proc utc*(dt: DateTime): DateTime =
1400 ## Shorthand for `dt.inZone(utc())`.
1401 dt.inZone(utc())
1402
1403 proc local*(dt: DateTime): DateTime =
1404 ## Shorthand for `dt.inZone(local())`.
1405 dt.inZone(local())
1406
1407 proc utc*(t: Time): DateTime =
1408 ## Shorthand for `t.inZone(utc())`.
1409 t.inZone(utc())
1410
1411 2 proc local*(t: Time): DateTime =
1412 ## Shorthand for `t.inZone(local())`.
1413

 1
1415 proc now*(): DateTime {.tags: [TimeEffect], gcsafe.} =
1416 ## Get the current time as a `DateTime` in the local timezone.
1417 ## Shorthand for `getTime().local`.
1418 ##
1419 ## .. warning:: Unsuitable for benchmarking, use `monotimes.getMonoTime` or
1420 ## `cpuTime` instead, depending on the use case.
1421 getTime().local
1422
1423 proc dateTime*(year: int, month: Month, monthday: MonthdayRange,
1424 hour: HourRange = 0, minute: MinuteRange = 0, second: SecondRange = 0,
1425 nanosecond: NanosecondRange = 0,
1426 zone: Timezone = local()): DateTime =
1427 ## Create a new `DateTime <#DateTime>`_ in the specified timezone.
1428 runnableExamples:
1429 assert $dateTime(2017, mMar, 30, zone = utc()) == "2017-03-30T00:00:00Z"
1430
1431 assertValidDate monthday, month, year
1432 let dt = DateTime(
1433 monthdayZero: monthday,
1434 year: year,
1435 monthZero: month.int,
1436 hour: hour,
1437 minute: minute,
1438 second: second,
1439 nanosecond: nanosecond
1440 )
1441 result = initDateTime(zone.zonedTimeFromAdjTime(dt.toAdjTime), zone)
1442
1443 proc initDateTime*(monthday: MonthdayRange, month: Month, year: int,
1444 hour: HourRange, minute: MinuteRange, second: SecondRange,
1445 nanosecond: NanosecondRange,
1446 zone: Timezone = local()): DateTime {.deprecated: "use `dateTime`".} =
1447 ## Create a new `DateTime <#DateTime>`_ in the specified timezone.
1448 runnableExamples("--warning:deprecated:off"):
1449 assert $initDateTime(30, mMar, 2017, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc()) == "2017-03-30T00:00:00Z"
1450 dateTime(year, month, monthday, hour, minute, second, nanosecond, zone)
1451
1452 proc initDateTime*(monthday: MonthdayRange, month: Month, year: int,
1453 hour: HourRange, minute: MinuteRange, second: SecondRange,
1454 zone: Timezone = local()): DateTime {.deprecated: "use `dateTime`".} =
1455 ## Create a new `DateTime <#DateTime>`_ in the specified timezone.
1456 runnableExamples("--warning:deprecated:off"):
1457 assert $initDateTime(30, mMar, 2017, 00, 00, 00, utc()) == "2017-03-30T00:00:00Z"
1458 dateTime(year, month, monthday, hour, minute, second, 0, zone)
1459
1460 proc `+`*(dt: DateTime, dur: Duration): DateTime =
1461 runnableExamples:
1462 let dt = dateTime(2017, mMar, 30, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1463 let dur = initDuration(hours = 5)
1464 doAssert $(dt + dur) == "2017-03-30T05:00:00Z"
1465
1466 (dt.toTime + dur).inZone(dt.timezone)
1467
1468 proc `-`*(dt: DateTime, dur: Duration): DateTime =
1469 runnableExamples:
1470 let dt = dateTime(2017, mMar, 30, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1471 let dur = initDuration(days = 5)
1472 doAssert $(dt - dur) == "2017-03-25T00:00:00Z"
1473
1474 (dt.toTime - dur).inZone(dt.timezone)
1475
1476 proc `-`*(dt1, dt2: DateTime): Duration =
1477 ## Compute the duration between `dt1` and `dt2`.
1478 runnableExamples:
1479 let dt1 = dateTime(2017, mMar, 30, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1480 let dt2 = dateTime(2017, mMar, 25, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
1481
1482 doAssert dt1 - dt2 == initDuration(days = 5)
1483
1484 dt1.toTime - dt2.toTime
1485
1486 proc `<`*(a, b: DateTime): bool =
1487 ## Returns true if `a` happened before `b`.
1488 return a.toTime < b.toTime
1489
1490 proc `<=`*(a, b: DateTime): bool =
1491 ## Returns true if `a` happened before or at the same time as `b`.
1492 return a.toTime <= b.toTime
1493
1494 proc `==`*(a, b: DateTime): bool =
1495 ## Returns true if `a` and `b` represent the same point in time.
1496 if not a.isInitialized: not b.isInitialized
1497 elif not b.isInitialized: false
1498 else: a.toTime == b.toTime
1499
1500 proc `+=`*(a: var DateTime, b: Duration) =
1501 a = a + b
1502
1503 proc `-=`*(a: var DateTime, b: Duration) =
1504 a = a - b
1505
1506 proc getDateStr*(dt = now()): string {.rtl, extern: "nt$1", tags: [TimeEffect].} =
1507 ## Gets the current local date as a string of the format `YYYY-MM-dd`.
1508 runnableExamples:
1509 echo getDateStr(now() - 1.months)
1510 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
1511 result = newStringOfCap(10) # len("YYYY-MM-dd") == 10
1512 result.addInt dt.year
1513 result.add '-'
1514 result.add intToStr(dt.monthZero, 2)
1515 result.add '-'
1516 result.add intToStr(dt.monthday, 2)
1517
1518 proc getClockStr*(dt = now()): string {.rtl, extern: "nt$1", tags: [TimeEffect].} =
1519 ## Gets the current local clock time as a string of the format `HH:mm:ss`.
1520 runnableExamples:
1521 echo getClockStr(now() - 1.hours)
1522 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
1523 result = newStringOfCap(8) # len("HH:mm:ss") == 8
1524 result.add intToStr(dt.hour, 2)
1525 result.add ':'
1526 result.add intToStr(dt.minute, 2)
1527 result.add ':'
1528 result.add intToStr(dt.second, 2)
1529
1530
1531 #
1532 # Iso week forward declarations
1533 #
1534
1535 proc initDateTime*(weekday: WeekDay, isoweek: IsoWeekRange, isoyear: IsoYear,
1536 hour: HourRange, minute: MinuteRange, second: SecondRange,
1537 nanosecond: NanosecondRange,
1538 zone: Timezone = local()): DateTime {.gcsafe, raises: [], tags: [], since: (1, 5).}
1539
1540 proc initDateTime*(weekday: WeekDay, isoweek: IsoWeekRange, isoyear: IsoYear,
1541 hour: HourRange, minute: MinuteRange, second: SecondRange,
1542 zone: Timezone = local()): DateTime {.gcsafe, raises: [], tags: [], since: (1, 5).}
1543
1544 #
1545 # TimeFormat
1546 #
1547
1548 when defined(nimHasStyleChecks):
1549 {.push styleChecks: off.}
1550
1551 type
1552 DateTimeLocale* = object
1553 MMM*: array[mJan..mDec, string]
1554 MMMM*: array[mJan..mDec, string]
1555 ddd*: array[dMon..dSun, string]
1556 dddd*: array[dMon..dSun, string]
1557
1558 when defined(nimHasStyleChecks):
1559 {.pop.}
1560
1561 type
1562 AmPm = enum
1563 apUnknown, apAm, apPm
1564
1565 Era = enum
1566 eraUnknown, eraAd, eraBc
1567
1568 ParsedTime = object
1569 amPm: AmPm
1570 era: Era
1571 year: Option[int]
1572 month: Option[int]
1573 monthday: Option[int]
1574 isoyear: Option[int]
1575 yearweek: Option[int]
1576 weekday: Option[WeekDay]
1577 utcOffset: Option[int]
1578
1579 # '0' as default for these work fine
1580 # so no need for `Option`.
1581 hour: int
1582 minute: int
1583 second: int
1584 nanosecond: int
1585
1586 FormatTokenKind = enum
1587 tkPattern, tkLiteral
1588
1589 FormatPattern {.pure.} = enum
1590 d, dd, ddd, dddd
1591 GG, GGGG
1592 h, hh, H, HH
1593 m, mm, M, MM, MMM, MMMM
1594 s, ss
1595 fff, ffffff, fffffffff
1596 t, tt
1597 yy, yyyy
1598 YYYY
1599 uuuu
1600 UUUU
1601 V, VV
1602 z, zz, zzz, zzzz
1603 ZZZ, ZZZZ
1604 g
1605
1606 # This is a special value used to mark literal format values.
1607 # See the doc comment for `TimeFormat.patterns`.
1608 Lit
1609
1610 TimeFormat* = object ## Represents a format for parsing and printing
1611 ## time types.
1612 ##
1613 ## To create a new `TimeFormat` use `initTimeFormat proc
1614 ## <#initTimeFormat,string>`_.
1615 patterns: seq[byte] ## \
1616 ## Contains the patterns encoded as bytes.
1617 ## Literal values are encoded in a special way.
1618 ## They start with `Lit.byte`, then the length of the literal, then the
1619 ## raw char values of the literal. For example, the literal `foo` would
1620 ## be encoded as `@[Lit.byte, 3.byte, 'f'.byte, 'o'.byte, 'o'.byte]`.
1621 formatStr: string
1622
1623 TimeParseError* = object of ValueError ## \
1624 ## Raised when parsing input using a `TimeFormat` fails.
1625
1626 TimeFormatParseError* = object of ValueError ## \
1627 ## Raised when parsing a `TimeFormat` string fails.
1628
1629 const
1630 DefaultLocale* = DateTimeLocale(
1631 MMM: ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct",
1632 "Nov", "Dec"],
1633 MMMM: ["January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June", "July",
1634 "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"],
1635 ddd: ["Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"],
1636 dddd: ["Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday", "Saturday",
1637 "Sunday"],
1638 )
1639
1640 FormatLiterals = {' ', '-', '/', ':', '(', ')', '[', ']', ',', '.'}
1641
1642 proc `$`*(f: TimeFormat): string =
1643 ## Returns the format string that was used to construct `f`.
1644 runnableExamples:
1645 let f = initTimeFormat("yyyy-MM-dd")
1646 doAssert $f == "yyyy-MM-dd"
1647 f.formatStr
1648
1649 proc raiseParseException(f: TimeFormat, input: string, msg: string) =
1650 raise newException(TimeParseError,
1651 "Failed to parse '" & input & "' with format '" & $f &
1652 "'. " & msg)
1653
1654 proc parseInt(s: string, b: var int, start = 0, maxLen = int.high,
1655 allowSign = false): int =
1656 var sign = -1
1657 var i = start
1658 let stop = start + min(s.high - start + 1, maxLen) - 1
1659 if allowSign and i <= stop:
1660 if s[i] == '+':
1661 inc(i)
1662 elif s[i] == '-':
1663 inc(i)
1664 sign = 1
1665 if i <= stop and s[i] in {'0'..'9'}:
1666 b = 0
1667 while i <= stop and s[i] in {'0'..'9'}:
1668 let c = ord(s[i]) - ord('0')
1669 if b >= (low(int) + c) div 10:
1670 b = b * 10 - c
1671 else:
1672 return 0
1673 inc(i)
1674 if sign == -1 and b == low(int):
1675 return 0
1676 b = b * sign
1677 result = i - start
1678 else:
1679 result = 0
1680
1681 iterator tokens(f: string): tuple[kind: FormatTokenKind, token: string] =
1682 var i = 0
1683 var currToken = ""
1684
1685 template yieldCurrToken() =
1686 if currToken.len != 0:
1687 yield (tkPattern, currToken)
1688 currToken = ""
1689
1690 while i < f.len:
1691 case f[i]
1692 of '\'':
1693 yieldCurrToken()
1694 if i.succ < f.len and f[i.succ] == '\'':
1695 yield (tkLiteral, "'")
1696 i.inc 2
1697 else:
1698 var token = ""
1699 inc(i) # Skip '
1700 while i < f.len and f[i] != '\'':
1701 token.add f[i]
1702 i.inc
1703
1704 if i > f.high:
1705 raise newException(TimeFormatParseError,
1706 "Unclosed ' in time format string. " &
1707 "For a literal ', use ''.")
1708 i.inc
1709 yield (tkLiteral, token)
1710 of FormatLiterals:
1711 yieldCurrToken()
1712 yield (tkLiteral, $f[i])
1713 i.inc
1714 else:
1715 # Check if the letter being added matches previous accumulated buffer.
1716 if currToken.len == 0 or currToken[0] == f[i]:
1717 currToken.add(f[i])
1718 i.inc
1719 else:
1720 yield (tkPattern, currToken)
1721 currToken = $f[i]
1722 i.inc
1723
1724 yieldCurrToken()
1725
1726 proc stringToPattern(str: string): FormatPattern =
1727 case str
1728 of "d": result = d
1729 of "dd": result = dd
1730 of "ddd": result = ddd
1731 of "dddd": result = dddd
1732 of "GG": result = GG
1733 of "GGGG": result = GGGG
1734 of "h": result = h
1735 of "hh": result = hh
1736 of "H": result = H
1737 of "HH": result = HH
1738 of "m": result = m
1739 of "mm": result = mm
1740 of "M": result = M
1741 of "MM": result = MM
1742 of "MMM": result = MMM
1743 of "MMMM": result = MMMM
1744 of "s": result = s
1745 of "ss": result = ss
1746 of "fff": result = fff
1747 of "ffffff": result = ffffff
1748 of "fffffffff": result = fffffffff
1749 of "t": result = t
1750 of "tt": result = tt
1751 of "yy": result = yy
1752 of "yyyy": result = yyyy
1753 of "YYYY": result = YYYY
1754 of "uuuu": result = uuuu
1755 of "UUUU": result = UUUU
1756 of "V": result = V
1757 of "VV": result = VV
1758 of "z": result = z
1759 of "zz": result = zz
1760 of "zzz": result = zzz
1761 of "zzzz": result = zzzz
1762 of "ZZZ": result = ZZZ
1763 of "ZZZZ": result = ZZZZ
1764 of "g": result = g
1765 else: raise newException(TimeFormatParseError,
1766 "'" & str & "' is not a valid pattern")
1767
1768 proc initTimeFormat*(format: string): TimeFormat =
1769 ## Construct a new time format for parsing & formatting time types.
1770 ##
1771 ## See `Parsing and formatting dates`_ for documentation of the
1772 ## `format` argument.
1773 runnableExamples:
1774 let f = initTimeFormat("yyyy-MM-dd")
1775 doAssert "2000-01-01" == "2000-01-01".parse(f).format(f)
1776

1905 ✗ proc parsePattern(input: string, pattern: FormatPattern, i: var int,
1906 ✗ parsed: var ParsedTime, loc: DateTimeLocale): bool =
1907 1 template takeInt(allowedWidth: Slice[int], allowSign = false): int =
1908 ✗ var sv = 0
1909 ✗ var pd = parseInt(input, sv, i, allowedWidth.b, allowSign)
1910 1 if pd < allowedWidth.a:
1911 6 return false
1912 6 i.inc pd
1913 sv
1914
1915 template contains[T](t: typedesc[T], i: int): bool =
1916 i in low(t)..high(t)
1917
1918 result = true
1919
1920 case pattern
1921 of d:
1922 let monthday = takeInt(1..2)
1923 parsed.monthday = some(monthday)
1924 result = monthday in MonthdayRange
1925 of dd:
1926 let monthday = takeInt(2..2)
1927 parsed.monthday = some(monthday)
1928 result = monthday in MonthdayRange
1929 of ddd:
1930 result = false
1931 for d, v in loc.ddd:
1932 if input.substr(i, i+v.len-1).cmpIgnoreCase(v) == 0:
1933 parsed.weekday = some(d.WeekDay)
1934 result = true
1935 i.inc v.len
1936 break
1937 of dddd:
1938 result = false
1939 for d, v in loc.dddd:
1940 if input.substr(i, i+v.len-1).cmpIgnoreCase(v) == 0:
1941 parsed.weekday = some(d.WeekDay)
1942 result = true
1943 i.inc v.len
1944 break
1945 of GG:
1946 # Assumes current century
1947 var isoyear = takeInt(2..2)
1948 var thisCen = now().year div 100
1949 parsed.isoyear = some(thisCen*100 + isoyear)
1950 result = isoyear > 0
1951 of GGGG:
1952 let isoyear = takeInt(1..high(int))
1953 parsed.isoyear = some(isoyear)
1954 result = isoyear > 0
1955 of h, H:
1956 parsed.hour = takeInt(1..2)
1957 result = parsed.hour in HourRange
1958 of hh, HH:
1959 parsed.hour = takeInt(2..2)
1960 result = parsed.hour in HourRange
1961 of m:
1962 parsed.minute = takeInt(1..2)
1963 result = parsed.hour in MinuteRange
1964 of mm:
1965 parsed.minute = takeInt(2..2)
1966 result = parsed.hour in MinuteRange
1967 of M:
1968 let month = takeInt(1..2)
1969 result = month in 1..12
1970 parsed.month = some(month)
1971 of MM:
1972 let month = takeInt(2..2)
1973 result = month in 1..12
1974 parsed.month = some(month)
1975 of MMM:
1976 result = false
1977 for n, v in loc.MMM:
1978 if input.substr(i, i+v.len-1).cmpIgnoreCase(v) == 0:
1979 result = true
1980 i.inc v.len
1981 parsed.month = some(n.int)
1982 break
1983 of MMMM:
1984 result = false
1985 for n, v in loc.MMMM:
1986 if input.substr(i, i+v.len-1).cmpIgnoreCase(v) == 0:
1987 result = true
1988 i.inc v.len
1989 parsed.month = some(n.int)
1990 break
1991 of s:
1992 parsed.second = takeInt(1..2)
1993 of ss:
1994 parsed.second = takeInt(2..2)
1995 of fff, ffffff, fffffffff:
1996 let len = ($pattern).len
1997 let v = takeInt(len..len)
1998 parsed.nanosecond = v * 10^(9 - len)
1999 result = parsed.nanosecond in NanosecondRange
2000 of t:
2001 case input[i]:
2002 of 'P':
2003 parsed.amPm = apPm
2004 of 'A':
2005 parsed.amPm = apAm
2006 else:
2007 result = false
2008 i.inc 1
2009 of tt:
2010 if input.substr(i, i+1).cmpIgnoreCase("AM") == 0:
2011 parsed.amPm = apAm
2012 i.inc 2
2013 elif input.substr(i, i+1).cmpIgnoreCase("PM") == 0:
2014 parsed.amPm = apPm
2015 i.inc 2
2016 else:
2017 result = false
2018 of yy:
2019 # Assumes current century
2020 var year = takeInt(2..2)
2021 var thisCen = now().year div 100
2022 parsed.year = some(thisCen*100 + year)
2023 of yyyy:
2024 let year =
2025 if input[i] in {'+', '-'}:
2026 takeInt(4..high(int), allowSign = true)
2027 else:
2028 takeInt(4..4)
2029 result = year > 0
2030 parsed.year = some(year)
2031 of YYYY:
2032 let year = takeInt(1..high(int))
2033 parsed.year = some(year)
2034 result = year > 0
2035 of uuuu:
2036 let year =
2037 if input[i] in {'+', '-'}:
2038 takeInt(4..high(int), allowSign = true)
2039 else:
2040 takeInt(4..4)
2041 parsed.year = some(year)
2042 of UUUU:
2043 parsed.year = some(takeInt(1..high(int), allowSign = true))
2044 of V:
2045 let yearweek = takeInt(1..2)
2046 parsed.yearweek = some(yearweek)
2047 result = yearweek in IsoWeekRange
2048 of VV:
2049 let yearweek = takeInt(2..2)
2050 parsed.yearweek = some(yearweek)
2051 result = yearweek in IsoWeekRange
2052 of z, zz, zzz, zzzz, ZZZ, ZZZZ:
2053 case input[i]
2054 of '+', '-':
2055 let sign = if input[i] == '-': 1 else: -1
2056 i.inc
2057 var offset = 0
2058 case pattern
2059 of z:
2060 offset = takeInt(1..2) * 3600
2061 of zz:
2062 offset = takeInt(2..2) * 3600
2063 of zzz, ZZZ:
2064 offset.inc takeInt(2..2) * 3600
2065 if pattern == zzz:
2066 if input[i] != ':':
2067 return false
2068 i.inc
2069 offset.inc takeInt(2..2) * 60
2070 of zzzz, ZZZZ:
2071 offset.inc takeInt(2..2) * 3600
2072 if pattern == zzzz:
2073 if input[i] != ':':
2074 return false
2075 i.inc
2076 offset.inc takeInt(2..2) * 60
2077 if pattern == zzzz:
2078 if input[i] != ':':
2079 return false
2080 i.inc
2081 offset.inc takeInt(2..2)
2082 else: assert false
2083 parsed.utcOffset = some(offset * sign)
2084 of 'Z':
2085 parsed.utcOffset = some(0)
2086 i.inc
2087 else:
2088 result = false
2089 of g:
2090 if input.substr(i, i+1).cmpIgnoreCase("BC") == 0:
2091 parsed.era = eraBc
2092 i.inc 2
2093 elif input.substr(i, i+1).cmpIgnoreCase("AD") == 0:
2094 parsed.era = eraAd
2095 i.inc 2
2096 else:
2097 result = false
2098 of Lit: raiseAssert "Can't happen"
2099
2100 proc toDateTime(p: ParsedTime, zone: Timezone, f: TimeFormat,
2101 input: string): DateTime =
2102 var year = p.year.get(0)
2103 var month = p.month.get(1).Month
2104 var monthday = p.monthday.get(1)
2105 year =
2106 case p.era
2107 of eraUnknown:
2108 year
2109 of eraBc:
2110 if year < 1:
2111 raiseParseException(f, input,
2112 "Expected year to be positive " &
2113 "(use 'UUUU' or 'uuuu' for negative years).")
2114 -year + 1
2115 of eraAd:
2116 if year < 1:
2117 raiseParseException(f, input,
2118 "Expected year to be positive " &
2119 "(use 'UUUU' or 'uuuu' for negative years).")
2120 year
2121
2122 let hour =
2123 case p.amPm
2124 of apUnknown:
2125 p.hour
2126 of apAm:
2127 if p.hour notin 1..12:
2128 raiseParseException(f, input,
2129 "AM/PM time must be in the interval 1..12")
2130 if p.hour == 12: 0 else: p.hour
2131 of apPm:
2132 if p.hour notin 1..12:
2133 raiseParseException(f, input,
2134 "AM/PM time must be in the interval 1..12")
2135 if p.hour == 12: p.hour else: p.hour + 12
2136 let minute = p.minute
2137 let second = p.second
2138 let nanosecond = p.nanosecond
2139
2140 if monthday > getDaysInMonth(month, year):
2141 raiseParseException(f, input,
2142 $year & "-" & ord(month).intToStr(2) &
2143 "-" & $monthday & " is not a valid date")
2144
2145 if p.utcOffset.isNone:
2146 # No timezone parsed - assume timezone is `zone`
2147 result = dateTime(year, month, monthday, hour, minute, second, nanosecond, zone)
2148 else:
2149 # Otherwise convert to `zone`
2150 result = (dateTime(year, month, monthday, hour, minute, second, nanosecond, utc()).toTime +
2151 initDuration(seconds = p.utcOffset.get())).inZone(zone)
2152
2153 proc toDateTimeByWeek(p: ParsedTime, zone: Timezone, f: TimeFormat,
2154 input: string): DateTime =
2155 var isoyear = p.isoyear.get(0)
2156 var yearweek = p.yearweek.get(1)
2157 var weekday = p.weekday.get(dMon)
2158
2159 if p.amPm != apUnknown:
2160 raiseParseException(f, input, "Parsing iso weekyear dates does not support am/pm")
2161
2162 if p.year.isSome:
2163 raiseParseException(f, input, "Use iso-year GG or GGGG as year with iso week number")
2164
2165 if p.month.isSome:
2166 raiseParseException(f, input, "Use either iso week number V or VV or month")
2167
2168 if p.monthday.isSome:
2169 raiseParseException(f, input, "Use weekday ddd or dddd as day with with iso week number")
2170
2171 if p.isoyear.isNone:
2172 raiseParseException(f, input, "Need iso-year with week number")
2173
2174 let hour = p.hour
2175 let minute = p.minute
2176 let second = p.second
2177 let nanosecond = p.nanosecond
2178
2179 if p.utcOffset.isNone:
2180 result = initDateTime(weekday, yearweek.IsoWeekRange, isoyear.IsoYear, hour, minute, second, nanosecond, zone)
2181 else:
2182 result = (initDateTime(weekday, yearweek.IsoWeekRange, isoyear.IsoYear, hour, minute, second, nanosecond, zone).toTime +
2183 initDuration(seconds = p.utcOffset.get())).inZone(zone)
2184
2185 2 proc format*(dt: DateTime, f: TimeFormat,
2186 loc: DateTimeLocale = DefaultLocale): string {.raises: [].} =
2187 ## Format `dt` using the format specified by `f`.
2188 runnableExamples:
2189 let f = initTimeFormat("yyyy-MM-dd")
2190 let dt = dateTime(2000, mJan, 01, 00, 00, 00, 00, utc())
2191 doAssert "2000-01-01" == dt.format(f)
2192
1/2
✓ Branch 0 taken 2 times.
✗ Branch 1 not taken.
 2 assertDateTimeInitialized dt
2193 2 result = ""
2194
0/2
✗ Branch 0 not taken.
